brief_document.title: Test harness: simulated time, activity mocking, replay assertions
brief_document.purpose: Ship the Temporal-equivalent test harness so authors can test workflows without an engine. Simulate time so a month-long sleep resolves instantly, mock activities with canned typed results, and assert a workflow replays to the same observations — all in pure gleam test.
brief_document.task: Implement aion/testing and its submodules: a TestEnv binding the ffi surface to in-Gleam test implementations (no beamr, engine, or store); testing/clock.gleam (advance resolves pending sleeps/timers instantly); testing/mock.gleam (activity mock registry intercepting workflow.run); testing/replay.gleam (run-twice-over-history replay assertions). Author workflow code must be unchanged between test and production.

requirements.title: TestEnv and the test-side ffi binding
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a TestEnv that runs entirely under gleam test with no beamr, engine, or store, binding the ffi surface to in-Gleam test implementations so author workflow code is unchanged between test and production.
acceptance: aion/testing exposes a TestEnv constructed in a test, holding the simulated clock, the mock registry, and the observation capture, stored in process-scoped state (process dictionary or an ETS table keyed by the running pid) so concurrent tests do not collide.

The swap mechanism is concrete: a hand-written test-only Erlang module test/aion_flow_ffi.erl implements every binding the production NIF namespace 'aion_flow_ffi' exposes, in-process against the current process's TestEnv. gleam test compiles and loads it so the identical @external(erlang, "aion_flow_ffi", ...) declarations resolve to it; the package ships NO aion_flow_ffi module in src/ (in production the engine registers those NIFs at runtime, per D2). Author workflow code and every @external declaration are byte-identical between test and production — only which module backs the name at load time differs.
A doc comment states the harness requires no external services (CO11) and is the recommended way to test workflows.
A test runs a trivial workflow under a TestEnv and asserts it completes with the expected typed result.

requirements.title: Simulated clock
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a simulated clock whose advance(Duration) resolves pending sleeps and timers instantly, with no wall-clock wait.
acceptance: testing/clock.gleam implements a logical clock; advance(env, Duration) moves logical time forward and resolves any sleep/timer whose deadline is now reached, returning control with no wall-clock sleep.
workflow.now under the harness returns the simulated clock's current logical time (deterministically), not wall time.
A test sleeps for days(30), advances the clock, and asserts the workflow proceeds instantly (the test itself runs in milliseconds).

requirements.title: Activity mock registry
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define an activity mock registry whose mock(name, fn(input) -> Result(output, ActivityError)) intercepts workflow.run for that activity and returns the canned typed result without dispatching a NIF.
acceptance: testing/mock.gleam lets a test register mock(env, name, fn(input) -> Result(output, ActivityError)); when the workflow calls workflow.run on an activity of that name, the mock's typed result is returned instead of a NIF dispatch.
The mock is type-checked against the activity's input/output types so a wrong-typed mock fails to compile.
A test mocks an activity to return a canned result and asserts the workflow under the env observes it; a second mocks a Retryable failure and asserts the workflow sees the typed ActivityError.

requirements.title: Replay assertions
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a replay assertion that runs a workflow over a recorded observation sequence twice and asserts the two observation sequences are identical, catching accidental non-determinism.
acceptance: testing/replay.gleam captures the observation sequence of a workflow run under the env, replays the workflow against that captured history, and asserts the replayed observation sequence is identical.

A test with a deterministic workflow passes the replay assertion; a test with a deliberately non-deterministic workflow (e.g. branching on an un-recorded value) fails the assertion with a clear diagnostic.
A doc comment relates the assertion to AD's non-determinism detection: the harness surfaces in test what the engine would surface as a NonDeterminismError in production.

boundaries: SHALL NOT require a running engine, beamr, or store — the harness is pure Gleam (CO11).
SHALL NOT implement the production engine replay/determinism — it simulates the observable contract for tests; the real machinery is AD.
SHALL NOT change the author-facing API; workflow code is identical in test and production.
verification: gleam test passes for the clock, mock, and replay-assertion tests, all running with no external services and in milliseconds (no wall-clock waits).
No panic/todo; a non-deterministic workflow fails the replay assertion with a diagnostic.

checklist.text: A TestEnv runs entirely under gleam test with no beamr, engine, or store, binding the ffi surface to in-Gleam test implementations.
The simulated clock's advance(Duration) resolves pending sleeps and timers instantly, with no wall-clock wait.
mock(name, fn(input) -> Result(output, ActivityError)) intercepts workflow.run for that activity and returns the canned typed result without dispatching a NIF.
A replay assertion runs a workflow over a recorded observation sequence twice and asserts the two observation sequences are identical, catching accidental non-determinism.

stories.text: As a test author, I want to advance simulated time so that a workflow which sleeps for a month is exercised instantly in gleam test, with no wall-clock wait.
As a test author, I want to mock an activity with a canned typed result so that I can test workflow logic without dispatching real side effects or running an engine.
As a test author, I want to assert that a workflow replays to the same observations so that I catch accidental non-determinism before it reaches production.

constraints.text: Pure Gleam package. No Rust, no engine code, no beamr dependency inside aion_flow. It publishes to Hex standalone (gleam_stdlib is its only runtime dependency, plus gleam_json for the default JSON codecs).
Every @external(erlang, ...) declaration lives in the single internal module aion/internal/ffi. No author-facing module declares an @external (per D1).

Module files contain logic; the package root and any mod-equivalent re-export aggregator stay declarations/re-exports only. No god files — split before a module exceeds ~500 lines of code (excluding comments/whitespace).

No partial implementations. Every public function is complete and total: no panic, no todo, no assert-as-control-flow; failure paths return typed Result errors. Gleam has no exceptions for control flow — every fallible binding returns Result.

Type safety is enforced, not advisory: activity input/output, signal payload, query return, and child input/output types are static type parameters; an invalid composition fails `gleam build`. A type-safety test brief asserts representative invalid compositions are rejected (compile-fail expectations documented).

Determinism: the SDK exposes workflow.now/workflow.random bound to AD's determinism context and exposes NO binding to a wall clock or entropy source. Workflow-code documentation states the prohibition explicitly.

The recorded/side-effectful boundary is structural: the only side-effect dispatch the SDK offers is workflow.run(activity) (and all/race/map over activities). There is no generic side_effect(fn) escape hatch (per D4).

No hardcoded defaults for configurable values: no default RetryPolicy, no default timeout, no default backoff numbers baked into the SDK. An activity without a retry decorator runs once; the author supplies all policy values (CLAUDE.md no-assumed-defaults).
User data crosses the boundary only through a Codec(a); a decode failure is a typed DecodeError, never a panic. No primitive silently swallows a decode error.

aion_flow does not implement engine mechanics (timer firing, signal routing, replay) — those are AE/AD/AT, reached via @external. It does not author NIFs in Rust — that is AN. It is not packaged into .aion — that is AP. The cluster owns only the Gleam API surface and its binding declarations.
All acceptance uses Gleam tooling: gleam build, gleam check, gleam format --check, gleam test must all pass clean. The test harness (aion/testing) runs under gleam test with no external services.

resolved_context.intention: Define aion_flow, the Gleam package (published to Hex) that workflow authors import to write durable workflows. It is the human-facing surface of the entire engine: a workflow author writes a plain, statically-typed Gleam function — decisions, loops, data transformation — and reaches the outside world only through this SDK's activity, signal, query, timer, and child-workflow primitives. The package is pure Gleam with no engine code inside it; every primitive is a thin @external(erlang) binding that resolves at runtime, when beamr loads the compiled workflow and the engine has registered the matching NIFs (AE-004), to the engine-side mechanics in AE/AD/AT. The defining property is compile-time type safety: activity inputs and outputs, signal payloads, and query returns are all statically typed, so an invalid composition — passing a String where an activity expects an Order, awaiting a signal of the wrong type, returning the wrong type from a query handler — fails at `gleam build`, not three days into a long-running production workflow. This is the concrete advantage over Temporal's SDKs (Go weakly typed, TypeScript better but runtime-checked). The package also ships aion/testing, the Temporal-equivalent test harness: simulate time so a month-long sleep resolves instantly, mock activities to return canned results, and assert that a workflow replays to the same state — all in plain gleam test, with no engine, no beamr, and no store.
